data.title: My Personal Account: Three Years of Losing Tens of Millions in Crypto VC – A Heartbreaking Lesson
data.description:
Author: BruceLLBlue

Recently, Twitter has been buzzing with posts from Chinese-speaking KOLs showing off how much they "earned in the past year": millions, tens of millions, 102.4 billion (don't run away if you're just making jokes)... After seeing all this, all I can say is, amazing! But as a former VC investment manager (GP, General Partner), I just want to complain: After working in Crypto VC for several years, I lost tens of millions of dollars. This isn't just empty talk; it's a real history of blood and tears. 3 years, 55+ investments, 27 losses (including rugs), 15 total losses, and I even invested in 9 relatively top-tier VCs.

Among them, NFT-related projects were completely wiped out, with GameFi suffering a 33% loss, and Infra being the hardest hit, with many projects reduced to only 10%-20% of their original valuation. Congratulations to the KOLs and esteemed crypto traders who are showing off their income; you caught the secondary market boom. But what about the ordinary VCs focused on primary market investments? They're constantly praising project teams; waiting 3-4 years for unlocking their investments, often ending up with "invested too early, invested correctly, but unable to exit." Why the opposite of what we call losses? Because this isn't about complaining about being poor, but a wake-up call. Crypto VC is inherently difficult; bear markets are brutal, and bull markets are often "harvested" by project teams. But I believe that in this new cycle, continuing as a VC (or evolving into one) may not be the best time. Although large institutional funds are entering the market, regulations are becoming clearer, and AI + on-chain tools are reshaping exit paths, I believe there are better ways and paths to realize self-worth. I'm sharing my hard-earned lessons for everyone's benefit. Lesson 1: The Naked Statistics: The Truth About the "Win Rate" of 55 Deals From joining Crypto VC in August 2022 to leaving in July 2025, I personally handled 55 direct investments and invested in 9 funds. Rugs accounted for 14/55 (25.45%): The hardest hit areas were NFT projects, all of which went to zero. One "star project" backed by a big IP experienced early NFT hype, but the team had little Web3 experience, and the founder, a top celebrity, showed little interest in issuing tokens. After the core members left, the project became a soft rug. Another "music + Web3" project, after leaving a giant company, worked on the project for several years, but achieved nothing and quietly died out. There are also Dex projects with "executives' entrepreneurial dreams": founders make their teams work illegally while they collect the money, and core employees run away; "potential stocks" from a university lab have basically all failed.

Losses account for 28/55 (50.1%): One GameFi project, after launching at 5x, ended up in a mess (only 20% of the cost remained, a 99% drop); another GameFi project, "produced by a team with a North American tech company background," peaked at 12x, but is now only 10% of its cost; yet another GameFi project was tricked by a CEX's Launchpad, causing a lot of token dumping pressure, and failed to gain traction.

The infrastructure sector fared even worse: no breakthroughs in the ecosystem, no technological innovation, and after the hype died down, many projects were left with only 10% of their original cost; without timely hedging, they were practically wiped out. Another example is the SocialFi project within the MOVE ecosystem, which collapsed just before the 2024 bull market. So what about fund of funds (FoF) investments? We invested in 9 top-tier European and American funds, including @hack_vc, @Maven11Capital, @FigmentCapital, @IOSGVC, and @BanklessVC. These funds have participated in the early-stage investments of many well-known projects during this cycle, such as @eigenlayer, @babylonlabs_io, @MorphoLabs, @movementlabsxyz, @ionet, @alt_layer, @MYX_Finance, @solayer_labs, @ethsign, @0G_labs, @berachain, @initia, @stable, @monad, @ether_fi, @brevis_zk, and @SentientAGI. On paper, the return looks decent at 2-3x, seemingly respectable, but the actual realized return (DPI) is estimated to be only 1-1.5x. Why this expectation? The main reason is the slow unlocking of projects and poor market liquidity. If a bear market or a crash similar to FTX occurs, the positions could be wiped out instantly. Lesson Two: The Deeper the Pit, the Deeper the Human Nature — Several Tragic Cases That Moved Me The most heartbreaking is the disastrous outcome of "investing in people": One Dex project, whose founder appeared to have the aura of a CEX executive, actually outsourced illegal labor to the team, pocketing the profits; GameFi's "North American giant dream" saw its price steadily decline after launching at 12x, never recovering. An Infra project founded by someone from @0xPolygon has no hope of ecosystem breakthrough, only retaining 15% of its initial investment valuation; several popular Infra projects launched on the two Korean giants (Upbit and Bithumb), then plummeted, never recovering. There was even a "music NFT" project founded by a Tencent Music executive who, after several years of playing the soft rug, achieved nothing. VCs in the Chinese-speaking region suffer even more: Language, mindset, and resources are inherent disadvantages. Western funds operate on a completely different model; they focus on scale to earn management fees, while we operate with short-sighted quick flips and paper-hands. Well-known projects, after raising huge sums, outsource the implementation of roadmaps globally (I've contacted several; as long as the funding is sufficient), and the founders only need to build the community and raise more money. VCs, on the other hand, are the most vulnerable group. Some projects airdrop their assets, trading via USB drives and Korean exchanges (they pump and dump the profits after reaching the target price, which is why Korean exchanges often have opening premiums). Investors have no way to verify this. Every VC thinks they're amazing; checking these funds' IRR and DPI is less worthwhile than investing in USDT/USDC fixed deposits. Lesson Three: After Losing So Much, I Learned the Evolution of "Exit is King" Being a VC is truly difficult. You have to survive bear markets, gamble on people's hearts, understand human nature, and wait for your coins to unlock. It's a 3-4 year cycle, and without hedging/liquidity management in the secondary market, excess returns are virtually impossible. In my experience, most projects that earned excess returns were invested in at the end of 2022-2023 after the FTX crash. The core reasons were: low project valuations, strong founder conviction, and the right investment timing (the project had enough time to explore and achieve TGE early when the bull market arrived). Why did other projects perform poorly or even lose money? The main reasons were either too expensive, too early, or misaligned unlocking. Reflecting on this carefully, these are all valuable lessons! Moreover, with $BTC constantly hitting new highs, traditional giants and Wall Street are rushing in, and the window for ordinary people to get rich quick is actually narrowing. Institutional investment returns are converging with those of Web2 venture capital (it's unlikely we'll return to the era of rampant growth before 2021). The new generation of investors: They aren't necessarily VCs; they're more likely to be individual angel investors or super KOLs. Through their influence and resources, they can often obtain better unlocking solutions and higher-priced tokens than VCs. And it's not just about investing early and correctly; it's about capturing the entire chain: primary + secondary + options/convertible bonds + airdrop interaction + market making hedging + DeFi arbitrage. There are actually serious cognitive misalignments and differences between the East and West, which is a goldmine for arbitrage. Turning around and picking up the keyboard, I'm embarking on a dignified path of alpha creation through content output. My years in Crypto VC, where I lost tens of millions, taught me one thing: constantly fawning over project teams, waiting for token unlocks, and gambling on people's hearts and minds only earned me the lowly label of "VC dog" and the complaints of my backers. Project teams could secretly airdrop tokens while investors could only watch helplessly. Enough! Now, I've chosen to turn to writing: by typing away, I'll share industry insights and alpha predictions, no longer agonizing over waiting for project unlocks, but proactively positioning myself and seizing project opportunities. Compared to the passive waiting of VCs, this path is more dignified; I can freely output value, and the compounding effect comes from readers' trust and sharing. Ultimately, these past few years have taught me: Patience > Opportunity, Luck > Professionalism, FOMO = Suicide.

data.title: 2025 Annual Summary and 2026 Industry Outlook
data.description:
Authors: Binance Research (Moulik Nagesh, Joshua Wong, Michael JJ, Asher Lin Jiayong)

2025 was a year of both milestone achievements and divergent market performance for the crypto industry. The total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies surpassed $4 trillion for the first time, with Bitcoin (BTC) reaching a new all-time high. This was driven by continued institutional investment, progress in regulatory policies—particularly in the stablecoin sector—and the expanding availability of compliant investment products. Simultaneously, macroeconomic uncertainties stemming from monetary policy adjustments, trade frictions, and geopolitical risks dominated market trends throughout the year, causing significant price volatility in crypto assets and recurring risk aversion. This resulted in a trading range of approximately 76% for cryptocurrencies throughout the year, with the total market capitalization fluctuating wildly between $2.4 trillion and $4.2 trillion. Despite structural breakthroughs in market access and infrastructure development, the cryptocurrency market ultimately declined by approximately 7.9% for the year.

This result highlights that in 2025, the pricing logic of crypto assets is increasingly influenced by the macroeconomic environment and traditional financial cycles, rather than simply determined by the industry's own application implementation progress. From a macro perspective, the key words for 2025 are "data fog" and volatility. The market experienced a series of events during that year, including the change of government in the United States, the impact of "Tariff Liberation Day," and the government shutdown, all of which severely disrupted the accuracy of economic signals. Although the artificial intelligence (AI) concept boom and the Debt Ceiling and Budget Act (OBBBA) propelled Bitcoin prices to new highs in the early second half of the year, by the end of 2025, due to delays in the implementation of regulatory policies, cryptocurrency trends decoupled from the rebound of traditional assets. However, the market outlook for 2026 foreshadows a clear "reboot of risky assets," driven by the "three pillars of policy": globally synchronized monetary easing policies, large-scale fiscal stimulus mainly through cash handouts and tax rebates, and a new wave of regulatory deregulation. This shift is expected to drive the market's funding structure from being dominated by retail speculators to being dominated by institutional funds. Meanwhile, the potential implementation of the US Bitcoin strategic reserve will inject liquidity into the cryptocurrency market, helping the industry usher in a new round of expansion. The market performance of Bitcoin exhibits a significant structural divergence: the level of capital activity at the market level is at odds with the economic activity on the underlying blockchain. Although Bitcoin hit a new all-time high this year, its closing price fell slightly at the end of the year, underperforming gold and most major stock indices. However, its market capitalization remained stable at around $1.8 trillion, with its market dominance maintained between 58% and 60%. Even with weak price performance, the trend of funds concentrating on Bitcoin is intensifying: US spot ETFs saw net inflows of over $21 billion, and corporate holdings exceeded 1.1 million BTC, accounting for 5.5% of the total Bitcoin supply. The network security of Bitcoin continues to strengthen, with peak hashrate exceeding 1 ZH/second and mining difficulty increasing by approximately 36% year-on-year, data confirming miners' long-term investment confidence. In contrast, Bitcoin's on-chain activity has weakened: the number of active addresses decreased by approximately 16% year-over-year, the number of transactions failed to surpass the peak of the previous bull market, and speculative token trading only exhibited brief, non-sustainable pulse-like price movements. These signals collectively indicate that Bitcoin's liquidity supply, pricing mechanism, and market demand are increasingly being realized through off-chain financial channels and long-term holding behavior, with the underlying public chain playing only a secondary role. This trend further solidifies Bitcoin's positioning as a macro-financial asset, rather than a network centered on transaction functionality. In the public chain (L1) sector, market practice in 2025 proved that simple on-chain activity is not a reliable indicator of its economic value—many public chains failed to convert user traffic into transaction fee revenue, value capture capabilities, and sustained positive token performance. Meanwhile, the market structure of the public chain sector continues to concentrate on a few leading projects. Ethereum maintains its dominant position due to its active developer ecosystem, decentralized finance (DeFi) liquidity, and total asset value. However, its relative returns lag behind Bitcoin due to the shrinking transaction capacity of its underlying chain and the fee compression effect caused by Layer 2 networks. Solana, on the other hand, maintained high transaction throughput and daily active user scale throughout the year, achieving substantial growth in stablecoin issuance. Even after the speculative frenzy subsided, it still generated considerable protocol revenue and successfully obtained approval for a US spot ETF, further enhancing institutional accessibility. BNB Chain, leveraging market narratives and a large retail trading base, drove significant growth in on-chain spot and derivatives trading, stablecoin settlement volume, and Real-World Asset (RWA) deployments. Its native token, BNB, became the best-performing mainstream crypto asset of the year. The core lesson from the public blockchain landscape in 2025 is that the competitive advantage of public blockchains increasingly depends on their ability to monetize continuous cash flows—such as transactions, payments, or institutional settlements—rather than simply maximizing the number of transactions. In 2025, the Ethereum Layer 2 (L2) network ecosystem handled over 90% of Ethereum-based transactions. This achievement benefited from the increased block space resulting from protocol upgrades and the reduction in data availability (DA) costs. As transaction execution functions migrate off-chain, the core issue of industry focus shifted to whether the scale effect of Layer 2 networks could translate into sustained user stickiness, transaction fee revenue, and economic synergy with the underlying public chains. In reality, the Layer 2 network ecosystem exhibits significant differentiation: transaction volume, liquidity, and transaction fee revenue are highly concentrated in a few optimistic rolling networks, represented by Base and Arbitrum; meanwhile, some dedicated application chains with clear application scenarios and superior user experiences also performed exceptionally well. Most other Layer 2 network projects experienced a precipitous drop in user activity after the incentive policies were withdrawn. Zero-knowledge (ZK) rolling networks have made continuous progress in proof efficiency and decentralization, but still lag behind optimistic rolling networks by an order of magnitude in terms of total value locked (TVL) and transaction fee revenue. Currently, the core constraints facing the Layer 2 network sector include: market fragmentation caused by over a hundred rolling networks, diminishing marginal utility of incentive mechanisms, and uneven decentralization of sorters. In 2025, the DeFi industry took another crucial step towards "structural institutionalization," shifting its focus to improving capital efficiency and compliance. The total value locked in the industry stabilized at $124.4 billion, with a significant shift in its capital composition. The proportion of stablecoins and interest-bearing assets increased substantially, while the proportion of inflationary tokens continued to decline. That year, the total value locked in real-world assets (RWA) reached $17 billion, historically surpassing decentralized exchanges (DEXs), driven by the widespread adoption of tokenized government bonds and stocks. Simultaneously, the U.S. Genius Act provided a clear regulatory framework for stablecoins, pushing the total market capitalization of stablecoins past $307 billion, officially making them a core global settlement infrastructure. Functionally, decentralized finance (DeFi) has evolved into a cash flow creation giant, with annual protocol revenue soaring to $16.2 billion, comparable to mainstream traditional financial institutions. Governance tokens have thus transformed into "blue-chip assets" with stable returns. The dominance of on-chain transactions has been further strengthened, with the peak share of spot trading volume on decentralized and centralized exchanges approaching 20%. 2025 can be considered a "breakthrough year for mainstreaming" for stablecoins. Driven by the landmark regulatory clarity brought by the GENIUS Act and the entry of institutional funds, the total market capitalization of stablecoins surged by nearly 50%, exceeding $305 billion. Daily trading volume of stablecoins soared by 26% to $3.54 trillion, a figure far exceeding Visa's $1.34 trillion, fully demonstrating the superiority of stablecoins in the field of high-speed cross-border payments. The industry's growth is driven by a number of emerging leading projects: six new stablecoins—BUIDL, PYUSD, RLUSD, USD1, USDf, and USDtB—each with a market capitalization exceeding $1 billion, have brought a new competitive landscape and real-world application scenarios to the market. These developments have laid a solid foundation for the continued expansion of stablecoins in areas such as payments, savings, and fintech. Consumer-grade crypto applications are entering a critical development phase: blockchain infrastructure is maturing, and the industry's focus is shifting from technological research and development to real-world application implementation and user experience optimization. Leading this transformation are various digital banks and fintech platforms—both Web2 giants and native Web3 companies—that are rapidly evolving into full-featured banking service providers based on blockchain technology. Although the market enthusiasm for crypto games and social applications has cooled somewhat this year, the deep integration of blockchain technology with the global payments and fintech industries has laid a crucial foundation for the emergence of a new generation of native networks in these sectors. These new networks will be designed around transparency and verifiability in their underlying architecture. As the industry's focus shifts from infrastructure construction to application-driven growth, its core mission is also evolving: from simply pursuing decentralization to meticulously building systems that combine credibility and verifiability, thereby gaining the trust of both consumers and institutional users. In 2025, cutting-edge technology development focused on the integrated innovation of three major technologies: AI agents, on-chain payments, and decentralized collaboration of real-world infrastructure. The most groundbreaking advancement was the realization of internet-scale application of an agent payment system based on the HTTP native settlement standard (which reactivated the functionality of the HTTP 402 "Payment Request" response code), providing a pay-per-use monetization model for application programming interfaces (APIs), data services, and automated workflows. By the end of the year, the payment network had processed over 100 million transactions, with a cumulative transaction volume exceeding $30 million, and daily active transactions consistently exceeding 1 million, with over 90% of transactions initiated by agents. Meanwhile, Decentralized Entity Artificial Intelligence (DePAI), an extension of the Decentralized Physical Infrastructure Network (DePIN), is emerging, aiming to achieve collaborative scheduling of autonomous machines. However, the bottleneck for development in this field in 2025 is not the design of token economic models, but rather limitations imposed by data quality, the gap between simulation environments and real-world scenarios, high capital investment thresholds, and security and regulatory compliance requirements. In contrast, DeFAI and DeSci are still in the exploratory stage and have not yet demonstrated sustainable economic output capabilities compared to native payments by intelligent agents and early machine economic applications. The core characteristic of institutional cryptocurrency applications in 2025 is that crypto assets are no longer simply used as price exposure tools, but are deeply integrated into the core financial business processes of institutions. Commercial banks are accelerating the development of crypto asset-backed lending businesses, signifying that Bitcoin (and selected Ethereum) are being increasingly recognized by institutions as financial-grade collateral under custody and compliance frameworks. At the same time, the variety and structure of compliant cryptocurrency ETFs continue to expand, further solidifying ETFs as the preferred channel for institutional investment in crypto assets. Tokenized money market funds have become a mature application scenario in the field of real-world asset tokenization. Leveraging their advantages of fast settlement, high collateral liquidity, and strong auditability, they have rapidly risen to become on-chain cash equivalents. Meanwhile, the scale of corporate digital asset vaults (DATs) has expanded significantly, but the market performance in 2025 also highlighted the sustainability pressures facing this sector: the returns of leveraged vault products were lower than those of interest-bearing ETFs with simpler structures. This phenomenon indicates that institutional crypto asset applications are shifting from simple asset accumulation to infrastructure and return-oriented deployments. Global cryptocurrency regulatory frameworks are gradually moving towards complementarity and synergy amidst differentiated development: the US promoted industry innovation through the GENIUS Act in July, establishing the first federal-level stablecoin regulatory framework; the EU formally implemented the Crypto Asset Market Regulation (MiCA), enacting a strict licensing system; Hong Kong, with its Stablecoin Ordinance and supporting tax incentives, consolidated its position as a crypto asset hub; and Singapore introduced stricter compliance and licensing rules in June, further raising industry entry standards. At the international level, the implementation of the OECD's Crypto Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) has accelerated across countries, laying a standardized foundation for global crypto asset tax transparency and cross-border information exchange. Looking ahead to 2026, the development prospects of several key sectors are highly promising, and we expect significant breakthroughs in these areas within the year. These themes cover multiple narrative directions and sub-sectors, including the macro environment and Bitcoin trends, institutional application implementation, policy and regulatory evolution, stablecoin innovation, asset tokenization, decentralized trading, and prediction markets.

data.title: Wall Street is entering the prediction market with a $200,000 annual salary offer.
data.description:
Written by: Niu Keke, Deep Tide TechFlow
It's finally here. The prediction market, once built by political supporters, speculative retail investors, and "wool party" (a term referring to those who exploit loopholes for profit), is welcoming a group of silent but deadly new players.

According to a report in the Financial Times on Thursday, several well-known trading firms, including DRW, Susquehanna, and Tyr Capital, are building dedicated prediction market trading teams.

DRW posted job advertisements last week, offering traders with a base annual salary of up to $200,000 for "real-time monitoring and trading of active markets" on platforms such as Polymarket and Kalshi.

Options trading giant Susquehanna is recruiting prediction market traders, requiring them to be able to "detect incorrect fair value," identify "abnormal behavior" and "inefficiencies" in the prediction market, and is also building a dedicated sports trading team.

Crypto hedge fund Tyr Capital continues to recruit prediction market traders who are already running complex strategies. Data supports this expansionary ambition. Monthly trading volume surged from less than $100 million at the beginning of 2024 to over $8 billion in December 2025, with a record-breaking $701.7 million traded on January 12th. When the pool of funds becomes deep enough to support the size of giants, the entry of Wall Street becomes inevitable. Arbitrage First. In prediction markets, institutions and retail investors are playing completely different games. Retail investors often rely on fragmented information to predict individual events, which is essentially gambling, while institutional players focus on cross-platform arbitrage and structural market opportunities. In October 2025, Boaz Weinstein, founder of hedge fund Saba Capital Management, stated in a closed-door meeting that predictive markets allow portfolio managers to hedge investments with greater accuracy, especially regarding the probability of specific events. Standing next to Polymarket CEO Shayne Coplan, he said, “A few months ago, Polymarket showed a 50% probability of a recession, while the credit market showed a risk of about 2%. You can think of countless pairing trades that couldn’t be done before.” According to Weinstein, hedge fund managers can buy “no recession” contracts on Polymarket because the market perceives a 50% probability of recession, making the contract relatively cheap. Meanwhile, in the credit market, one can short some bonds or credit products that will plummet during an economic recession, because the credit market only accounts for a 2% probability of a recession, so these products are still highly priced. If an economic recession does occur, you might lose a small amount of money in the polymarket, but make a large profit in the credit market because those overvalued bonds will crash. If there is no recession, you might make money in the polymarket and suffer a small loss in the credit market, but overall you will still profit. The emergence of prediction markets has given traditional financial markets a completely new "price discovery tool." The arrival of a privileged class further tilts the balance. Susquehanna is Kalshi's first market maker and has reached a tournament contract agreement with Robinhood. Kalshi offers market makers numerous benefits: lower fees, special trading limits, and more convenient trading channels; specific terms have not been disclosed. The entry of market makers will quickly change the market. Previously, the prediction market often suffered from insufficient liquidity, especially for niche events. When you wanted to buy or sell large contracts, you might face large spreads or simply not find a counterparty. Professional institutions will quickly eliminate obvious pricing errors. For example, price differences for the same event on different platforms, or obviously unreasonable probability pricing, will be quickly smoothed out. This is not good news for retail investors. Previously, you might have found that "Trump winning" had a 60% probability on Polymarket and a 55% probability on Kalshi, allowing for simple arbitrage. In the future, such opportunities will essentially disappear. With Wall Street PhDs earning hundreds of thousands of dollars annually, future predictive contracts may also enter an era of specialization and diversification, moving beyond just single-event predictions. Examples include: 1. Multi-event combination contracts, similar to parlay betting in sports betting; 2. Time series contracts, predicting the probability of an event occurring within a specific time period; 3. Conditional probability products, determining the probability of B occurring if A occurs… Looking back at financial history, from forex to futures to cryptocurrencies, the development of every emerging market follows a similar trajectory: retail investors ignite the spark, eventually leading to institutional dominance. The prediction market is repeating this process. Technological advantage, capital size, and privileged access will ultimately determine who survives this game of probability. For retail investors, while there may still be a glimmer of hope in long-term predictions or niche areas, they must recognize reality: as Wall Street's sophisticated machines operate at full speed, the era of easily profiting from information asymmetry may be over.

data.title: Germany’s Second-Largest Bank DZ Bank Receives MiCA Approval to Launch MeinKrypto for Retail Crypto Trading
data.description:
DZ Bank Opens Retail Crypto Trading Through Cooperative Banks With MiCA Approval

DZ Bank, Germany’s second-largest lender by assets, has secured regulatory approval from the Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin) under the European Union’s Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) framework, setting the stage for a retail-focused crypto trading platform across the cooperative banking sector.

JUST IN: 🇩🇪 Germany’s second-largest bank DZ Bank gets approval for institutional Bitcoin and crypto trading.

Bullish 🚀 pic.twitter.com/MemVrk9W3k

— Bitcoin Magazine (@BitcoinMagazine) January 14, 2026

How Will MeinKrypto Change Retail Banking Access to Crypto

The newly approved platform, meinKrypto, is designed for customers of Volksbanken and Raiffeisenbanken, allowing them to buy, sell, and hold cryptocurrencies entirely within the regulated banking environment.

Integrated directly into the VR Banking App, the service provides wallets and access to trading bitcoin (BTC), ether (ETH), litecoin (LTC) and cardano (ADA).

DZ Bank acts as the central institution supporting the network, but each cooperative bank must submit its own MiCAR notification to BaFin before activating the platform for its customers.

Germany’s DZ Bank just opened crypto trading to millions of mainstream banking customers. Its new meinKrypto platform lets users buy and hold $BTC, $ETH, $LTC and $ADA directly in the VR Banking App — a major push to make crypto feel like regular finance 🚀…

— invezz.com (@InvezzPortal) January 14, 2026

Once approved and implemented, retail clients will be able to invest in digital assets without relying on external exchanges.

Who Will Provide Custody and Trading Services

The technical infrastructure for meinKrypto was developed by Atruvia, the IT provider for the cooperative financial group.

Custody of digital assets will be handled by Boerse Stuttgart Digital, while trade execution will be managed by EUWAX AG.

Each cooperative bank will determine individually whether to offer the service, allowing for a staggered rollout based on strategy and risk considerations.

Why Cooperative Banks Are Embracing Crypto Now

The move reflects rising interest among Germany’s cooperative banks to enter the crypto market.

A survey conducted by Genoverband in September 2025 found that more than 71% of cooperative banks were considering offering crypto services to retail clients, up from 54% the previous year.

Around one-third of those banks indicated plans to launch services within five months, highlighting tangible market demand.

Krypto zieht in die Bankenwelt ein.

Eine aktuelle Genoverband-Studie zeigt: 71 % der Volks- und Raiffeisenbanken befassen sich mit Krypto-Handel – ein klarer Sprung im Vergleich zu 54 % im Vorjahr.

🚀 Erste Banken wollen schon in wenigen Monaten starten.
⏳ Andere rechnen mit… pic.twitter.com/IclfviKe5Y

— KM Intelligent Investments (@KM_Investments) September 21, 2025

From Institutional Testing to Retail Adoption

DZ Bank has previously focused on institutional clients, offering digital asset services through partnerships with regulated market infrastructure providers such as Boerse Stuttgart Digital.

The MiCAR license now extends this approach to retail customers, signalling a shift from experimental offerings to integration into mainstream financial services.

Will Digital Assets Become Part of Everyday Banking

The rollout follows a decentralised model where each cooperative bank decides individually whether to provide access to meinKrypto.

Customers can manage digital assets entirely digitally, keeping all transactions within the regulated banking system.

Additional cryptocurrencies may be added over time, subject to regulatory review.

DZ Bank Joins European Stablecoin Initiative

Alongside meinKrypto, DZ Bank recently joined Qivalis, a European banking consortium developing a euro-denominated stablecoin.

The project, involving 11 banks, aims to support regulated payments and settlement for European businesses and consumers, targeting a market entry in the second half of 2026.

Together, the meinKrypto rollout and stablecoin initiative reflect a broader push by Germany’s cooperative banking sector to integrate digital assets into traditional financial infrastructure while remaining fully compliant under MiCAR regulations.

data.title: China’s Digital Yuan Architect Accused of $8M Crypto Bribery Scheme
data.description:
Hardware wallets expose hidden corruption

China’s former digital yuan architect, Yao Qian, is facing serious corruption charges after Chinese state media revealed that he allegedly accepted more than $8 million in crypto bribes while holding senior regulatory positions. The case exposes a striking irony: Yao is accused of using the very blockchain technology he helped develop to conceal illicit financial transactions from businessmen seeking regulatory favors.

State broadcaster CCTV aired a documentary on January 14 titled “Technology Empowering Anti-Corruption,” highlighting how investigators traced 2,000 Ethereum in 2018 from a businessman directly to Yao’s personal wallet. Beyond crypto, Yao is accused of hiding at least 22 million yuan in fiat through shell accounts and multiple blockchain addresses.

Investigators found three hardware wallets in Yao’s office drawer, each appearing like a standard USB device but holding millions in cryptocurrency. Zou Rong, a member of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection, noted

“These three seemingly insignificant little wallets stored tens of millions of yuan.”

Despite Yao’s belief that virtual currencies provided anonymity, the transparent nature of blockchain allowed authorities to reconstruct complete transaction histories, linking illicit funds to Yao’s personal accounts.

Among the assets purchased with these bribes was a Beijing villa valued at over 20 million yuan, partially funded by crypto-to-fiat conversions. Authorities also traced 12 million yuan through a businessman using multiple shell companies, revealing an elaborate network of hidden transactions.

Yao’s former subordinate, Jiang Guoqing, acted as the primary intermediary for these crypto bribes. Jiang acknowledged his role in facilitating the money laundering, saying

“I set up a transfer address where people would send coins, then transfer them to Yao Qian’s personal wallet.”

Investigators allege that in 2018, Jiang connected businessman Zhang to Yao, enabling Zhang’s company to issue tokens and raise 20,000 Ethereum through a crypto exchange in exchange for 2,000 Ethereum funneled to Yao.

Prosecutors also documented a pattern of extravagant perks beyond digital bribes, including luxury banquets, lavish gifts, influence over recruitment, and procurement deals with tech providers. Authorities even highlighted superstitious practices and relationships with individuals considered “key training targets” for illicit activity—behaviors at odds with Communist Party governance norms.

Evidence chain led to expulsion and prosecution

Yao was expelled from the Communist Party in November 2024 and transferred for criminal prosecution after investigators established a “closed loop of evidence”, combining blockchain transaction records with traditional financial forensics.

As investigators put it, “Cryptocurrency is useless if it can’t be cashed out—when virtual assets eventually become real assets, their true nature is easily exposed.” The unfinished Beijing villa, purchased with converted crypto, became a key physical manifestation of the elaborate digital deception spanning years of regulatory service.

🇨🇳China to allow Interest on Digital Yuan in major overhaul:

The People’s Bank of China (PBOC) is set to revamp its digital yuan framework, allowing banks to pay interest on #eCNY wallet balances and shifting its role beyond a cash substitute toward a deposit-like instrument.… pic.twitter.com/8X0s964w4k

— Urs Bolt 🇨🇭🇵🇭 (@UrsBolt) December 30, 2025

Despite this scandal, China’s central bank digital currency rollout continues. The People’s Bank of China implemented a new framework on January 1, 2026, allowing commercial banks to pay interest on digital yuan wallet balances.

While the e-CNY has processed 3.48 billion transactions totaling 16.7 trillion yuan through November 2025, it still trails far behind Alipay and WeChat Pay, which dominate over 90% of China’s mobile payments market. Yao’s downfall, while significant, does not appear to have derailed China’s long-term digital currency strategy.

data.title: Discord Loses Appeal for DeFi Communities as Teams Shift to Controlled Support
data.description:
The Digital Town Square Is Falling Silent

The era of the open-access crypto chat room is coming to an abrupt end as leading decentralised finance (DeFi) protocols begin to board up their digital doors.

For years, Discord served as the heartbeat of the industry, a place where developers and investors could mingle freely.

However, that openness has transformed the platform into what many now describe as a liability.

Morpho, a prominent lending protocol, recently highlighted this shift by announcing that its public server will transition to a read-only format on 1 February 2026.

Morpho is fully moving to traditional customer support, away from discord.

Crypto is def at the verge of a massive cultural shift rn pic.twitter.com/dLbBp2j279

— Deep (@deepitreal) January 14, 2026

This move marks a pivot away from the "always-on" chat culture that once defined the sector, as teams prioritise user safety over constant social engagement.

Why Are Scammers Overrunning Public Discord Servers?

The decision to retreat from Discord is driven by a relentless surge in cybercrime that even the most vigilant teams struggle to contain.

Morpho co-founder Merlin Egalite admitted the shift away from the platform was “not an easy choice,” yet it became unavoidable as users were targeted by phishing attacks while simply trying to find help.

Moving from Discord was not an easy choice BUT:
- Discord is actually full of scammers. people would get phished while actually searching for answers despite heavy monitoring, safeguards, etc.
- We've been testing intercom so far and it has made support 100x easier: instant… https://t.co/L0kvzB1YGm

— Merlin Egalite 🦋 (@MerlinEgalite) January 14, 2026

The pseudonymous founder of DefiLlama, 0xngmi, echoed these frustrations, revealing that his platform has been quietly reducing its reliance on the chat app for some time.

He argued that the very design of the platform makes it impossible to shield the community from bad actors, noting,

“Even if you ban scammers instantly, they still DM users directly to scam them.”

at defillama we've also been moving away from discord into other channels like live support chat & email tickets

discord makes it impossible to protect your users from getting scammed, even if you ban scammers instantly they still DM users directly to scam them https://t.co/ZLmw8yapFx

— 0xngmi is hiring (@0xngmi) January 14, 2026

New Tools For Secure Support And Documentation

Rather than maintaining chaotic chat rooms, builders are now migrating toward structured, professional support systems that resemble traditional financial services.

Morpho has been experimenting with Intercom, a tool that offers features like ticket management, instant translation, and AI-driven responses.

Merlin Egalite found that these tools streamlined support tasks while offering a much higher level of security than a public forum.

Today we had 4 big @intercom product announcements. Huge shoutout to the many talented people here who worked on all of these 🥰

* 1 New AI-first Messenger*

Messaging/Chat tools for Customer Service were never designed with AI at the centre, we added AI to what existed already.… pic.twitter.com/KRewd3aNYj

— Paul Adams (@Padday) June 27, 2024

Richard Rodairos, a talent partner at Dragonfly, views this professionalisation as a necessary evolution for the industry.

He described public servers as “one of the lowest signal surfaces in the space” and argued that both developers and users would benefit more from clear, asynchronous documentation.

Rodairos went as far as to suggest that the industry should “Get rid of most discords in crypto. It would actually do the industry good long term.”

Get rid of most discords in crypto. It would actually do the industry good long term.

Public discords have become one of the lowest signal surfaces in the space:

Serious users don’t need a 10k member chat room to get value. Real builders want clear docs, async support, and… pic.twitter.com/FlkJjPEXYI

— Richard (@Rodairos) January 14, 2026

Trading Social Engagement For Mental Health And Security

Beyond the technical risks, the human cost of managing these communities has become a point of contention.

Marc Zeller, the founder of Aavechan Initiative, described the platform as “full of scammers” and noted that users asking simple questions are frequently preyed upon by criminals posing as administrators.

He praised Morpho’s exit as a positive development and urged other major protocols to consider leaving the platform.

Discord is full of scammers and any user asking a question will get DMs from fake admins.
I think it's a difficult but good decision and Aave labs should also stop maintaining a discord as there's little upside. https://t.co/4sNK39dGbp

— Marc ”七十 Billy” Zeller (@Marczeller) January 14, 2026

For Duncan Cock Foster, co-founder of Nifty Gateway, the move is a logical one.

He shared that moderating a Discord server was the single most difficult part of running his former company and a major drain on his mental health, calling the decision to step away “smart.”

When I was running Nifty Gateway, tending to our discord was the worst part of the job, a huge drain on my mental health and incredibly distracting

Unless you're a pyscho who really enjoys it, you shouldn't have a discord as a crypto founder. Smart move by @Morphohttps://t.co/vITfwoIPOv

— Duncan Cock Foster (@dccockfoster) January 14, 2026

Is The Loss Of Community Interaction Worth The Safety?

Despite the security concerns, some observers fear that closing these channels will erode the collaborative spirit of the DeFi movement.

An X user known as Llamaonthebrink argued that the ability to “pop into a project’s Discord” to provide feedback or follow development discussions is a unique feature of the sector that should be preserved.

One of the allures of DeFi was that you could simply pop into a project’s discord and find fellow users who share their product experiences with each other, make suggestions, provide feedback, and track any development discussions.

I personally hope this doesn’t become a trend. https://t.co/1SiZAVpfN6

— MilliΞ (@llamaonthebrink) January 14, 2026

Other critics suggest the issue lies not with the software itself, but with how it is used.

Users like nft_dreww and Walter_Su11ivan contend that well-managed servers with proper security protocols can remain safe and vibrant.

They suggest that giving up on the platform is more a reflection of a team’s moderation skills than a flaw in the tool, pointing to several projects that continue to maintain healthy and secure communities.

I heavily disagree too. Completely giving up on using discord to foster communities is a skill issue. Plenty of servers that have well-ran groups. Most scams are basic, if people are this susceptible, they clearly don't have enough common-sense to do DeFi.https://t.co/ob58bTAVTY

— ⟢ nightmare kitten ⟣ 𓃠 (@Walter_Su11ivan) January 15, 2026

The Great Institutionalisation of Decentralised Support

Coinlive views this exodus as the final nail in the coffin for the "Wild West" era of crypto communication, where chaos was often mistaken for community.

By trading open chat rooms for ticket-based systems, DeFi protocols are effectively admitting that decentralisation cannot exist without centralised guardrails.

This pivot to professionalised, gated support represents a necessary maturity that mirrors traditional banking, yet it risks killing the organic p2p culture that birthed the movement.

The market is now forcing a choice: do we value the illusion of an open digital square, or the reality of a secure financial vault?

If protocols cannot protect their users in a simple chat room, it raises a larger question about their ability to protect billions in assets without slowly becoming the very institutions they sought to replace.

data.title: DeFi Quietly Exits Discord Amid Growing Scam Festering In Public Channels, But Is Leaving The Real Solution?
data.description:
DeFi quietly steps back from Discord as scams overwhelm public channels

Decentralized finance projects are increasingly distancing themselves from public Discord servers, arguing that the platform has become a security liability rather than a community hub as scams and phishing attacks continue to target users at scale.

The shift gained attention this week after DeFi lending protocol Morpho moved its public Discord server into read-only mode, redirecting users to alternative support channels.

Morpho's shutting down its public Discord. Didn't see that coming.

This could be a trend for other protocols too—scams, bot scraping, or just too much noise might be at play. But maybe its also signals that big DeFi teams are focusing more on institutions and less on… pic.twitter.com/k3UeCKXyaS

— Anton Cheng (@antonttc) January 14, 2026

While the move was not framed as a full exit, it underscored a growing unease among builders who say always-on chat rooms are becoming harder to secure without exposing users to bad actors.

According to Morpho co-founder Merlin Egalité, the decision to limit Discord access was not made lightly. He said users were being phished while actively seeking help, despite extensive monitoring, warning messages and automated moderation tools.

In reality, these monitoring and helplines become useless after scammers find a way to infiltrate inside and impersonate support staff and initiate private conversations and execute their deceitful schemes.

Egalité highlighted that the real core issue is more structural than anything. Even if malicious accounts are removed quickly, Discord allows attackers to directly message users who have interacted in public channels. That makes it difficult for teams to fully prevent fraud, especially when users assume that anyone contacting them after a support query is acting in an official capacity.

this discord scammer that messaged me randomly pic.twitter.com/UMI0ibEhdd

— Saber (@sab3r_official) January 8, 2026

Similar concerns have been raised by DefiLlama’s pseudonymous founder, known as 0xngmi, who said that its data platform has been quietly reducing its reliance on Discord in favor of more controlled communication methods. He argued that open servers make it nearly impossible to protect users, since scammers can continue to operate through private messages even after being banned from public channels.

Structured support replaces always-on chat

In response, some DeFi teams are experimenting with alternatives designed to limit exposure to impersonation and social engineering. Rather than relying on public chat rooms, projects are moving toward ticket-based help desks, live support widgets and email systems that funnel users through official, verifiable channels.

Morpho, for example, has been testing customer support tools such as Intercom, which offer features like ticket tracking, audit logs and AI-assisted responses. Builders say these systems make it easier to guide users through legitimate support flows while reducing reliance on informal conversations that can be exploited by attackers.

at defillama we've also been moving away from discord into other channels like live support chat & email tickets

discord makes it impossible to protect your users from getting scammed, even if you ban scammers instantly they still DM users directly to scam them https://t.co/ZLmw8yapFx

— 0xngmi is hiring (@0xngmi) January 14, 2026

For many teams, the shift reflects a broader change in priorities. Instead of maximizing engagement through constant real-time interaction, the focus is increasingly on reliability, traceability and user protection — even if that comes at the cost of a more open community environment.

Industry voices back the Discord pullback

Several founders, investors and community leaders have publicly supported the move away from public Discord servers. Richard Rodairos, a talent partner at Dragonfly, described crypto Discords as one of the lowest-signal communication surfaces in the industry. He argued that users and builders would benefit more from clear documentation, asynchronous support and dependable announcements than from chaotic real-time chats.

Marc Zeller, founder of the Aavechan Initiative, echoed those concerns, calling Discord “full of scammers” and praising Morpho’s decision. He urged other DeFi projects to reconsider whether open servers still serve users’ best interests.

Discord is full of scammers and any user asking a question will get DMs from fake admins.
I think it's a difficult but good decision and Aave labs should also stop maintaining a discord as there's little upside. https://t.co/4sNK39dGbp

— Marc ”七十 Billy” Zeller (@Marczeller) January 14, 2026

Former Nifty Gateway co-founder Duncan Cock Foster also weighed in, saying that moderating Discord was the most mentally draining aspect of running his company and describing the shift toward structured support as a sensible evolution.

When I was running Nifty Gateway, tending to our discord was the worst part of the job, a huge drain on my mental health and incredibly distracting

Unless you're a pyscho who really enjoys it, you shouldn't have a discord as a crypto founder. Smart move by @Morphohttps://t.co/vITfwoIPOv

— Duncan Cock Foster (@dccockfoster) January 14, 2026

Supporters of the move argue that fewer, better-managed communication channels could reduce user harm while easing the operational burden on teams already stretched thin.

Community pushback highlights DeFi’s core tension

Not everyone agrees that stepping back from Discord is the right solution. Critics warn that abandoning public servers risks undermining one of DeFi’s defining characteristics: open, peer-to-peer collaboration between users and builders.

Projects are leaving @discord and everyone’s trying to figure out why, but the explanations you’re hearing probably miss the actual point.

few say it’s about:
- bots and spam
- moderation challenges
- limitations or feature requests

But none of these actually explain why this… pic.twitter.com/UrOkybmkyb

— Srini (@srinivasteja) January 15, 2026

Some community members argue that the ability to quickly join a project’s Discord to ask questions, share experiences and follow development discussions is central to DeFi’s appeal. From this perspective, reducing Discord access could make projects feel less transparent and more distant from their communities.

Others contend that the issue lies not with Discord itself, but with how it is managed. They point out that scammers operate across all major social platforms and argue that well-moderated Discord servers with strict permissions, clear warnings and strong operational discipline can still function safely.

A middle ground begins to emerge

For now, DeFi appears to be testing a compromise. Rather than fully abandoning Discord, some projects are maintaining a limited public presence while routing sensitive issues and official support through controlled, verifiable channels. The approach reflects a maturing sector grappling with scale, security and responsibility.

As DeFi grows beyond its early adopter phase, the debate over Discord highlights a broader question facing the industry: how to preserve openness and community without exposing users to unnecessary risk. Whether Discord remains a cornerstone of DeFi culture or fades into a secondary role may depend on how well projects can balance those competing priorities.

data.title: Fact Check: How much money did the University of Chicago actually lose trading cryptocurrency?
data.description:
Author: Darren Terminator

Recently, Jiemian News took the opportunity of the publication of the third edition of Professor Zhao Dingxin's "Lectures on Social and Political Movements" (the second edition of this book is truly excellent) to interview Professor Zhao. In the interview, Professor Zhao stated that the University of Chicago's recent budget cuts were because "it is said that the university seems to have listened to the investment advice of some Nobel laureates, losing more than six billion US dollars in cryptocurrency trading. It can be said that the University of Chicago's reduction in liberal arts programs has nothing to do with Trump's policies."

So, did the University of Chicago really lose more than six billion US dollars in cryptocurrency trading?

Coincidentally, the University of Chicago mentioned cryptocurrency trading in its Q&A updated in December 2025[1]. According to the official website: "Contrary to a certain news report, the University of Chicago has not suffered losses on its cryptocurrency investments. Our investment in cryptocurrencies is relatively small, but it has more than doubled in the past five years. Our investment goal is to provide a stable source of income to support our programs in the long term and secure our future." So, is the University of Chicago's provost necessarily telling the truth? It's hard to say. However, intuitively speaking, the total amount of the University of Chicago's endowment fund in the last five years is about $10 billion (the highest in fiscal year 2021 was about $11.6 billion; and about $10.9 billion in fiscal year 2025 [2]). Unless the University of Chicago is really crazy enough to use at least 60% of its endowment fund to speculate on cryptocurrencies (which obviously violates various regulations), or misappropriates a large amount of operating funds to speculate on cryptocurrencies and loses it all, it should not have lost as much as six billion. So how much did it actually lose? Or did it actually make a fortune as the official Q&A said? The Stanford University newspaper [3], the Financial Times [4] and Investopedia [5] reported on this last year. According to the Stanford C.T. newspaper, their four sources indicated that "the University of Chicago lost tens of millions of dollars around 2021 due to its cryptocurrency investments." So what does the University of Chicago's financial report [6] say? Unfortunately, the financial report does not directly tell us exactly how much money was lost in cryptocurrency trading. However, in its 2022 fiscal year financial report, the University of Chicago disclosed its cryptocurrency investments (fair market value): approximately $64 million as of the end of June 2021, and as of June 2022... At the end of the month, it was approximately $45 million (the difference was approximately $19 million). In subsequent financial reports, perhaps due to excessive profits or losses, the University of Chicago changed its reporting method and stopped disclosing its cryptocurrency investments. However, according to a Q&A in 2025, the University of Chicago remained relatively cautious in its cryptocurrency investments. It's worth noting that the 2022 financial report showed that as of the end of June that year, the University of Chicago's endowment fund had suffered a total investment loss of approximately $1.5 billion. The 2023 financial report showed that the University of Chicago's investments only incurred a small loss. In the following two years, the University of Chicago's investment performance improved. However, we don't know exactly which of these losses and profits originated from cryptocurrency trading. The Stanford University student newspaper offered a less reliable clue: "[The University of Chicago's] target asset allocation data shows that the university's ideal allocation to private debt and 'absolute return' investments (including alternative assets such as cryptocurrencies) has decreased from 25.5% in 2020 to 20% in 2022, suggesting a significant withdrawal (or decline) of high-risk alternative assets." However, the Stanford University student newspaper also offered an interesting observation: "From 2013 to 2023, the University of Chicago's endowment fund had an annualized return of only 7.48%, while the stock market's annualized return was 12.8%, and the Ivy League schools' average was 10.8%. If the University of Chicago had simply followed market performance, its endowment fund would now be $6.45 billion more. And this (dreamlike) sum would be more than enough to pay off all of the school's debts." Of course, universities cannot simply replicate market indices, as they must hedge against economic downturns to maintain financial stability. But even if the University of Chicago were to reach the average level of its Ivy League peers, its endowment would still be $3.69 billion larger. This would be enough to cover the school’s current budget deficit for the next 15 years. However, besides cryptocurrency speculation and investment losses, what other reasons could explain the University of Chicago’s budget cuts? Common explanations, besides Trump being a rogue, often emphasize the University of Chicago’s own strategic mistakes: borrowing heavily, large-scale infrastructure projects, and aggressive expansion. [7][8] As of the end of June 2025, the University of Chicago’s debt was approximately $9.2 billion [9], about 90% of its endowment. Although the financing costs of these debts are relatively low compared to those across the ocean, the University of Chicago still needs to pay more than $200 million in interest this fiscal year. Such a high debt is certainly not out of thin air. Since the beginning of the new century, the University of Chicago has spent a lot of money on new laboratories, libraries, dormitories, and technology in order to enhance its reputation and enrollment, as well as to compete with various established universities. Moreover, these expansions are mostly supported by a large amount of debt. However, the new infrastructure brings continuous operating costs, but the school has not thought about how to support it in the long term. The University of Chicago newspaper[10] quoted Professor Clifford Ando as saying that any parents who want to send their children to the University of Chicago need to think about whether the tuition fees they have worked so hard to pay are paying for their children's education or paying for the school's debt. The crazy expansion and the debt problems that followed are obviously the responsibility of the school's management for being hot-headed and ambitious. Ironically, the president's base salary increased by 285% between 2006 and 2022. Now facing some economic problems, the management is shifting the burden onto students and faculty: even in years of selling assets, laying off staff, and halting enrollment, executive salaries continue to rise. So what should the University of Chicago do next? Besides continuing to cut costs, it must, of course, increase revenue. Clearly, a common tactic used by American universities to increase revenue is to enroll more undergraduates. The University of Chicago will do the same, though its reasons will certainly be presented in a high-sounding manner.

data.title: Behind the silver "short squeeze": Why is shorting it still dangerous?
data.description:
Author: BiyaNews

Recently, the silver market has been as hot as the summer heat. Looking at the SLV (iShares Silver Trust ETF) trend, many investors exclaimed, "This is practically the 'Nvidia' of precious metals!" When an asset rises at such a steep slope, the "fear of heights" and "contrarian thinking" factors in human nature begin to stir. I have friends who are itching to short it, betting on a pullback.

However, historical experience tells me that under the current macroeconomic landscape and microeconomic structure, shorting silver is tantamount to colliding head-on with a high-speed train. You might guess the direction correctly, but you're unlikely to survive until victory. Behind this is far more than just "what goes up must come down"; it's an extreme short squeeze orchestrated by multiple "trump card" factors.

Spot Trading "Flips the Table": When Financial Games Meet Real Demand

Traditionally, the precious metals trading system centered in London and New York has built a vast world of derivatives. In this world, $100 worth of physical silver in a warehouse may correspond to several times or even dozens of times more futures and options contracts. This is essentially a credit-based financial game.

But the rules of the game are being rewritten.

In recent years, physical demanders, represented by major global manufacturing centers, have exhibited a clear attitude: "Forget the abstract, I want physical delivery." When industrial users and investors are no longer satisfied with paper contracts and demand physical delivery, the vulnerability of the entire system is laid bare. According to publicly available market reports and warehousing data, COMEX (New York Mercantile Exchange) silver inventories have shown signs of tightness several times in recent years. Short sellers in the futures market face a dilemma at expiration: either buy silver at a high price for delivery or close their positions. Both actions drive up prices. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle: rising prices → triggering more delivery demand or short covering → further pushing up prices → inventory appears even tighter. This price surge driven by physical delivery pressure is a "black swan" event that is difficult for technical analysis to predict. I recall the short squeeze at GameSpot (GME) in early 2021, triggered by retail investors. Its core logic was similar: a short squeeze. When short positions are too crowded and the available circulating supply (or physical commodity) is insufficient, any buying pressure can trigger a stampede of short covering. While the silver market isn't entirely driven by retail investors, the underlying logic of "spot trading is king" means that any large-scale short selling risks being "physically" disproven. Industrial "Silver Devourers": Demand Restructuring Under AI and the Green Revolution If a financial short squeeze is "adding fuel to the fire," then profound changes in fundamental demand are the "dried tinder" burning ever brighter in the furnace. Silver is not a scarce metal, but its demand structure is undergoing a silent revolution. For a long time, silver has been known for its investment properties and jewelry demand. However, it is easily overlooked that it is also an extremely important industrial metal. Silver possesses the highest electrical and thermal conductivity of all metals, making it irreplaceable in high-end manufacturing. Currently, two major trends are reshaping the industrial demand landscape for silver: Artificial Intelligence and Electrification: The explosive growth of AI data centers, high-performance computing chips, and 5G/6G infrastructure has created massive demand for high-end connectors, switches, and conductors. Every server and every base station is silently consuming silver. Green Energy Revolution: The photovoltaic industry is the absolute growth engine for silver demand. The silver paste on the front of each solar panel is the core conductive material. Despite technological advancements aimed at reducing unit consumption ("finer lines," "less silver"), the exponential growth in global photovoltaic (PV) installations has completely offset these savings. Data from organizations like the International Energy Agency (IEA) continues to revise upwards forecasts for future PV installations, indicating a robust engine for "green demand" for silver. The supply side is equally compelling. More than half of the world's silver is produced as a byproduct of metals such as copper, lead, zinc, and gold. This means that silver supply is not independent and heavily reliant on mining investment and extraction plans for other base metals. When major mining companies slow investment due to costs, environmental policies, or unfavorable expectations for base metal prices, silver supply growth is naturally limited. A more nuanced point is that the world's largest silver producer and a major industrial consumer has shown a stronger strategic control over key mineral resources, including silver, in recent years. While specific policy details require consulting the latest official documents, a major trend is that countries are placing greater emphasis on using resources to safeguard their domestic supply chains rather than simply exporting for foreign exchange. This rise of "resource nationalism," in the long run, may further tighten global silver trade flows and exacerbate regional supply-demand mismatches. The "Hidden" Geopolitical Manifesto: Silver's Return to its Monetary Role? In investment, some of the most powerful drivers are often hidden beneath the headlines. The current complex geopolitical landscape is, in a classical way, reawakening silver's ancient attribute—that of a monetary metal. When traditional international payment systems (such as SWIFT) become unusable due to sanctions, transacting parties must find an alternative medium with high consensus, stable value, and ease of cross-border transfer. Gold is certainly the first choice, but its high unit price is not conducive to small- to medium-sized trade settlements. This is where silver's advantages become apparent. Market analysis points out that in trade between countries restricted by international sanctions and their trading partners, silver is being used as a form of "physical currency" or collateral. For the recipient, silver is both a hard asset with intrinsic value and can be easily integrated into their domestic industrial chain (such as India's huge silver jewelry processing and consumption market) or resold to the global market. This process essentially creates a new "enclave" of physical silver demand within the gaps of international trade, independent of the dollar system. This demand is hidden and difficult to quantify precisely, yet it is real. Like ocean currents beneath the surface, though unseen, it continuously and powerfully influences the direction of the sea. This provides a solid "hidden support" for silver prices, with low correlation to global financial market fluctuations.

Lessons for Investors: Be Afraid of Heights, But Don't Go Against the Trend

Faced with such a complex confluence of positive factors, how should we respond?

First, understand the essence of the market. The current silver market is not simply a speculative bubble, but the result of multiple resonances of financial conditions, industry cycles, and geopolitics. The biggest risk of shorting is that you are fighting against a fundamental situation supported by a real spot shortage, strong industrial demand, and strategic hoarding. This is different from shorting an overvalued tech stock, which may only face the risk of liquidity contraction.

Second, respect the power of trends. My experience is that in the early stages of a trend, you can discuss valuations; but in the mid-to-late stages of a trend's acceleration, especially when accompanied by structural changes (such as the aforementioned spot market run and demand revolution), discussing "where the top is" is often more meaningful than discussing "when the reversal will occur."

Trying to predict how high lava will erupt during a volcanic eruption is dangerous and futile. So, how should ordinary investors participate? For trend traders: Consider ETFs like SLV, but always set strict stop-loss orders. The end of a trend often sees dramatic fluctuations; entry timing and position management are more important than directional judgment. For long-term investors: Consider allocating a small portion of silver as a commodity asset to hedge against currency devaluation and geopolitical risks, in a diversified portfolio, rather than as a tool for short-term speculation. For everyone: If you feel prices are high and you don't know where to start, the best strategy is to wait and see. The market never lacks opportunities, it lacks patience. As the old saying goes: "Missing out" isn't a risk, "making a mistake" is. The story of silver is a microcosm of the clash between the old world's financial order and the new world's industrial demands, and also a vivid lesson in risk education: never short sell simply because prices have "gone too high." The real risk lies in ignoring the underlying forces driving prices up. In the market's frenzy, sometimes the wisest move is neither to join in nor to fight back, but to stand aside and first understand the rhythm of the music. After all, the prerequisite for making money is protecting your principal.

data.title: China Bans More Than A Dozen Western Cybersecurity Companies As It Pushes For Tech Self-Reliance
data.description:
China Moves To Ban Western Cybersecurity Firms Over National Security Concerns

Chinese authorities have instructed domestic companies to stop using cybersecurity software from more than a dozen U.S. and Israeli firms, citing concerns that Western technology could transmit sensitive information abroad.

The move is part of Beijing’s broader drive for technological self-reliance under its “Xinchuang” policy.

China has added domestic AI chips from Huawei and Cambricon to its official government procurement “Xinchuang” list for the first time, steering public sector IT spend toward local GPUs pic.twitter.com/hlaZaBByo8

— The FinanceX (@thefinancexnews) December 10, 2025

Which Companies Are Affected By The Ban

The ban targets major American firms including Broadcom-owned VMware, Palo Alto Networks, Fortinet, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Rapid7, McAfee, Recorded Future, Claroty, and Alphabet-owned Mandiant and Wiz.

Israeli firms on the list include Check Point Software Technologies, Orca Security, Cato Networks, and CyberArk, which was recently acquired by Palo Alto Networks.

Investors in these companies need to pay close attention to the impact of China's ban.

US:Microsoft/Cisco/Palo Alto/Fortinet/CrowdStrike/Broadcom/VMware/Zscaler/Okta
Israel:Check Point/CyberArk/Wiz/Orca Security/Aqua Security/SentinelOne/Cato Networks/Claroty/Cyera/Radware/Snyk https://t.co/6APbckc1Wd

— LimSurb (@limsurb) January 15, 2026

Imperva, now owned by French defence company Thales, is also affected.

Several of the banned firms operate significant local offices.

Fortinet maintains three offices in mainland China and one in Hong Kong, Broadcom has six locations, and Palo Alto Networks operates five offices, including one in Macau.

Other firms, like CrowdStrike and SentinelOne, said they do not sell directly to Chinese clients and would be minimally affected.

CrowdStrike $CRWD and SentinelOne $S : These two are a useful contrast case because Reuters reported both companies said they do not really sell into China, with CrowdStrike saying it does not sell into China and has no offices, hires, or infrastructure there.😅

— VibeCheckInvest (@VibecheckInvest) January 15, 2026

Why China Is Targeting Western Cybersecurity Software

Beijing has expressed growing concern that Western software could be exploited for espionage.

According to sources familiar with the matter said,

“Chinese authorities expressed concern the software could collect and transmit confidential information abroad.”

The move follows repeated allegations by some of the banned companies that Chinese hackers targeted foreign governments and diplomats.

Check Point recently reported a Chinese-linked operation against a European government office, while Palo Alto Networks claimed Chinese hackers targeted diplomats worldwide.

China-linked #InkDragon expands into Europe, building a distributed relay network by weaponizing compromised servers with a custom #ShadowPad IIS listener alongside new TTPs and an evolved FinalDraft.https://t.co/HiVSN2rngc

— Check Point Research (@_CPResearch_) December 16, 2025

China has denied these allegations.

Domestic Alternatives Are Being Prioritised

Local firms are being encouraged to switch to Chinese cybersecurity providers such as 360 Security Technology and Neusoft.

Analysts note that this mirrors actions taken by the U.S., which banned Russian software like Kaspersky Lab from government networks in 2017 and again restricted its products in 2024.

The US is set to join a host of other countries restricting popular antivirus software developer #Kaspersky Lab's software due to concerns about Russian government influence.

Find out more about the ban and how you can better protect your data 👉https://t.co/hnnXeqi4N5pic.twitter.com/MXDsHEHCL7

— Proton (@ProtonPrivacy) August 15, 2024

Impact On Companies And Markets

The announcement affected share prices of some of the blacklisted firms.

Broadcom shares fell more than 5%, Fortinet dropped around 2%, and Palo Alto Networks declined slightly.

Check Point shares rose marginally.

Companies like CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, and Recorded Future clarified they do business minimally in China, while others maintain extensive operations.

China’s Push For Technology Independence

The ban reflects Beijing’s push to reduce reliance on foreign technology amid growing U.S.-China competition in semiconductors, AI, and cybersecurity.

Chinese state-owned enterprises are also being pressured to limit the use of Western hardware and consulting services.

The timing coincides with preparations for a visit by U.S. President Donald Trump to Beijing in April, highlighting how technological and trade tensions continue to influence China’s domestic policies.

China’s strategy demonstrates a clear focus on national security and technological sovereignty, steering domestic companies away from foreign software and encouraging investment in local alternatives.
